  • Code files and their use
    Chapter 1: Learning from data . . . Chapter 2: Generalizing from models Chapter 3: Multiple linear regression Chapter 4: Exploiting the linear model framework Chapter 5: Generalized linear models and survival analysis Chapter 6: Time series models Chapter 7: Multilevel models, and repeated measures Chapter 8: Tree-based Classification and Regression Chapter 9: Multivariate data exploration and discrimination Appendix A: The R System – A Brief Overview Code for Selected Figures Create static html files for posting to GitHub

PGRcode

PGRcode.Rmd
suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(PGRcode))

This website is designed to make available code and other supplementary materials for a new Cambridge University Press text, due to appear in print in May or June 2024.

“A Practical Guide to Data Analysis Using R – An Example-Based Approach”,
by John H Maindonald, W John Braun, and Jeffrey L Andrews.

Chapter and appendix headings are:

  • Chapter 1: Learning from data, and tools for the task
  • Chapter 2: Generalizing from models
  • Chapter 3: Multiple linear regression
  • Chapter 4: Exploiting the linear model framework
  • Chapter 5: Generalized linear models and survival analysis
  • Chapter 6: Time series models
  • Chapter 7: Multilevel models, and repeated measures
  • Chapter 8: Tree-based Classification and Regression
  • Chapter 9: Multivariate data exploration and discrimination
  • Appendix A: The R System – A Brief Overview

Major changes from the earlier text are

  • There is much more limited tutorial content on R syntax and semantics (readers are encouraged to refer to the wealth of online material.)
  • There is more critical attention to \(p\)-values
  • The use of Bayes Factors, contrasting them with \(p\)-values for the different and complementary perspective that they provide.
  • Ihere is greater attention to effect size issues.
  • There is extended commentary on the insights that replicability/reproducability studies provide.
  • Simulation and resampling approaches get more extended use – as sources of insight, as devices for building intuition, and as mechanisms for obtaining sampling distributions when theoretical results are not available.
  • There is new content on quantile regression with automatic choice of smoothing parameter, and on fitting monotonic increasing or decreasing response curves as specific forms as shape constrained additive response.
  • The treatment of models that allow for extra-binomial or extra-Poisson variation has been substantially extended.
  • Exponential time series (ETS) get greater attention, especially for their use in forecasting. Modeling of seasonal terms now gets attention.
  • A new section treats hierarchical and other forms of clustering.
  • The treatment of causal inference from observational data has been greatly extended.
  • There is some limited attention to the use of multiple imputation to fill in missing values in data where some observations are incomplete.

More detailed commentary is provided in “A Practical Guide – Style and Content”.

Developed by John H Maindonald, W John Braun, Jeffrey L Andrews.

Site built with pkgdown 2.0.7.
